author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
Is the Soul Deformed by the Body’s Imperfections?
title: Is the Soul Deformed by the Body’s Imperfections?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, again, what means it that you say, “The soul acquires form from the body, and grows and extends with the increase of the body,” without keeping in view what a monstrosity the soul of either a young man or an old man would become if his arm had been amputated when he was an infant?

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: “The hand of the soul,” you say, “contracts itself, so that it is not amputated with the hand of the body, and by condensation it shrinks into other parts of the body.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: At this rate the aforesaid arm of the soul will be kept, wherever it holds its ground, as short as it was at first when it received the form of the body, because it has lost the form by the growth of which it might itself have increased at an equal degree of expansion.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Thus the soul of the young man or the old man who lost his hand in his infancy advances with two hands, indeed (because the one which shrank back escaped the amputation of the bodily limb), but one of these was the hand of an adult, young or old, according to the hypothesis, while the other was only an infant’s hand, just as it was when the amputation happened.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Such souls, believe me, are not made in the mould and form of the body, but they are fictitiously framed under the deformed stamp of error.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: It seems to me impossible for you to be rescued from this error, unless with God’s help you fully and calmly examine the visions of those who dream, and from these convince yourself that some forms are not real bodies, but only the semblances of bodies.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: Now, although even those objects which we suppose to be like bodies are of the same class, yet so far as the dead are concerned, we can form an after guess about them from persons who are asleep.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Soul and its Origin.
verses.text: For it is not in vain that Holy Scripture describes as “asleep” those who are dead were it only because in a certain sense “sleep is akin to death.”
